html:
AQA Language Paper 2 Question 5 — 10 Most Likely Questions + Skeleton Plans (2026)

For: AQA GCSE English Language, Paper 2 Section B Question 5 (8700/2). 40 marks — AO5 (Communication and Organisation) 24 marks; AO6 (Technical Accuracy) 16 marks. 45-minute writing time. Target on the page: 650+ words.

Source. The themes below are distilled from the twelve verified AQA P2 Q5 sittings between June 2017 and June 2024 (see The Complete Writer's Craft — Creative Non-Fiction, Verified Past-Paper Writing Prompts section, for the full table of statements, forms, audiences, and paired reading sources). The forecast applies a recurrence-cycle analysis to AQA's own cluster pattern plus current-affairs salience for a 2026 candidate.

Statistics and sources. Every numeric claim and named source cited in the skeleton plans below has been verified against UK government, regulator, charity, or peer-reviewed sources (Ofcom, NHS, IFS, Sport England, WRAP, JISC, Mental Health Foundation, Royal Society for Public Health, Our World in Data, UNEP and similar). AQA examiners explicitly penalise invented statistics; everything here is real and citeable in exam conditions. If a claim seems too neat, check the citation before using it.

Variety of openings — no two model answers start the same way. A Grade-9 answer is not just a correctly structured IUMVCC; it opens in a way that is creative and fits the topic. There is no single "correct" opening. The model essays below deliberately use a different hook archetype each time, so you can see the range available to you — and so that the document never teaches one formula. The hooks below are the ones Sophicly teaches: a question, an "imagine" scenario, a metaphor, an anecdote, a shocking statistic, a bold statement, a contrast, an assumption-then-reversal. Pick whichever genuinely suits the statement AQA prints — not whichever you rehearsed. Forcing a clever opening onto the wrong topic loses more than it gains; the opening must earn its place.

Opening-hook map — what each model answer demonstrates (so you see the full range):

Q Topic Hook archetype used What it demonstrates
Q1 Social media Conversational taxonomy / direct address An opening that thinks aloud and sorts the topic into types
Q2 Climate Extended metaphor (the topic as a machine) A long, controlled sentence that builds one central image
Q3 Zoos Assumption set up, then reversed "You have heard… in fact the opposite is true"
Q4 Education / school hours Measured definition opening Calm, complete sentences that build a case brick by brick
Q5 Sport Concrete contrast ("Picture two people…") Inequality made visible by placing two images side by side
Q6 AI in education Assumption set up, then reversed A second reversal — same shape, different topic, different feel
Q7 Tourism Extended metaphor (scale and appetite) A second metaphor opening — shows the technique flexes
Q8 Career / vocation Conversational taxonomy + light humour Sorting "two types of jobs", with a deliberately bathetic list
Q9 Body image Historical contrast (then vs now) Two eras placed side by side to expose what has changed
Q10 Consumer ethics Repetition / anaphora "We reduce. We repair. We refuse." — rhythm as persuasion

These are illustrations, not prescriptions. The same topic could open with a shocking statistic, a rhetorical question, or a one-line anecdote and score just as highly — provided the choice fits. Study the variety; copy the range, not any single example. Each essay still runs IUMVCC underneath. The architecture does not change; the opening does. That variety is what inventive structure (AO5 top band) rewards.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

  1. A predicted theme for AQA P2 Q5 in 2026, with three plausible stem variants (different form / audience / statement combinations).
  2. A frame — the controlling argument that holds your whole answer together.
  3. Three hook options so you can pick the archetype that suits the stem AQA actually prints.
  4. A skeleton IUMVCC plan with sentence-level guidance for every beat.
  5. Three counter-argument variants so the strongest objection that surface prompt invites is the one you concede.
  6. A voice and banned-patterns check to run before you submit.

The plan structure stays identical across all ten questions. You rehearse the shell once; on the day, you swap in the surface prompt's audience, form, and statement of opinion. This is the same discipline that powers your Literature answers — memorise the load-bearing structure, recombine for the printed question.

Full IUMVCC walkthrough: Sophicly Persuasive Writing Guide.

Memorise this once — the IUMVCC plan shape

Sophicly's transactional persuasive structure has SIX beats. Memorise the beat name, the word target, and the dominant move for each one.

Beat Words Sentences Dominant move
I — Introduction 50–100 2–3 Hook + topic bridge. No facts list. Build an image.
U — Urgency 100–150 3–4 Metaphor-first. Why this matters NOW.
M — Methodology 250–350 9–12 (three sub-points) Each sub-point: point → metaphor → development → evidence. Organic transitions only.
V — Vision 100–150 3 Three escalating sentences painting the better future.
C — Counter-argument 75–100 2–3 Concede the strongest objection. Refute with one decisive pivot.
C — Conclusion 75–100 2–3 Circular echo of the Introduction image, or a climactic final picture.

Total on the page: ~650–850 words. AO5 reward = inventive structure that does not feel formulaic. AO6 reward = controlled vocabulary, sentence variety, accurate SPaG.

The eight Sophicly hooks (pick ONE per piece)

AQA reward variety; pick the archetype that suits the audience the stem specifies.

  1. Counterintuitive fact — a number that overturns assumption.
  2. Personal anecdote — a two-sentence concrete memory.
  3. Sensory snapshot — a single image written like a paragraph from a novel.
  4. Imagine scenario — Picture this: + a future or hypothetical moment.
  5. Rhetorical question — a question only the reader can answer.
  6. Bold statement — a claim, no qualifier.
  7. Contrast / paradox — two clauses that crash against each other.
  8. Extended metaphor — a single comparison the rest of the piece returns to.

MADFATHER'S CROPS — the floor, not the ceiling

You need two or three devices working together at every persuasive moment. Single-device sentences score low on AO5. MADFATHER'S CROPS is an aide-memoire — it lists the techniques you should never forget, not every technique you are allowed to use.

The MADFATHER'S CROPS aide-memoire:

  • M Metaphor / A Alliteration / D Direct address / F Facts + foreshadowing / A Assonance / T Triadic structure / H Hyperbole / E Emotive language / R Rhetorical questions / C Contrast / R Repetition / O Onomatopoeia / P Personification / S Simile + sibilance.

Beyond MADFATHER'S CROPS — the wider technique bank. Top-band AO5 answers reach for techniques the aide-memoire does not list. Learn the names and the shapes; use them where the topic invites them.

  • Anaphora — repeated word at the start of consecutive clauses ("We have the science. We have the funding. We have the time we are not using."). Repetition that builds pressure.
  • Epistrophe — repeated word at the END of consecutive clauses ("Reduce the bag. Repair the shelf. Refuse the bag.").
  • Polysyndeton — repeated "and" (or another conjunction) connecting items ("The kit and the strip and the seat and the ticket and the broadcast."). Slows the reader down; emphasises accumulation.
  • Asyndeton — list with the conjunctions deliberately removed ("Reduce, repair, refuse."). Speeds the reader up; emphasises compression.
  • Polysyllabic listing — a list of multi-syllable words for cumulative weight ("relentless, industrialised, unregulated, profitable.").
  • Parallelism — same grammatical shape repeated ("Move the bell. Move the calendar. Move the system."). Anaphora + parallelism is a Grade-9 combination.
  • Antithesis — two ideas balanced against each other in matched grammar ("The factory pays the wage; the wage pays the worker; the worker pays the factory back in attention.").
  • Chiasmus — A-B-B-A reversal ("Cages built for animals are now animals built for cages.").
  • Zeugma — one verb governing two objects, one literal and one figurative ("She lost her phone, her sleep, and her concentration in a single term.").
  • Climax / cumulative listing — list ordered by escalating intensity ("The bedroom dim. The classroom drifting. The decade lost.").
  • Bathos — sudden drop from the elevated to the trivial ("We are losing the polar ice cap, the Great Barrier Reef — and, on a less serious note, the Cornish coastline in August.").
  • Periodic sentence — the main point withheld until the end ("Despite the algorithms, the platforms, the engineered loneliness and the absent parents — childhood survives.").
  • Cumulative sentence — main point first, then escalating detail ("The phone is the problem — the phone, the platform behind it, the algorithm behind the platform, and the boardroom behind the algorithm.").
  • Anastrophe — inverted word order ("Long the country has waited.").
  • Aporia — feigned uncertainty as rhetorical move ("What does one even say to a generation raised on filters?").
  • Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person, idea or object ("Reader, you have done your share. The bin is not your fault.").
  • Litotes — emphasis via understated negative ("This is not a small problem.").
  • Anadiplosis — last word of one clause becomes first word of the next ("We confess to the bin. The bin confesses to the lorry. The lorry confesses to Malaysia.").

The point. A Grade-9 answer does not just use techniques — it picks the technique that fits the IDEA. Anaphora suits mounting pressure; asyndeton suits decisive action; bathos suits ironic deflation; chiasmus suits paradox; polysyndeton suits oppressive accumulation. Match the technique to the meaning.

Effects on the Reader — Sophicly's 4-fold framework

Every beat in IUMVCC has a target effect on the reader:

  • I — Focus. What should the reader notice first?
  • U — Feel. What emotion grips them now?
  • M — Think. What argument is reshaping their judgement?
  • V — Feel + Act. What better future moves them?
  • C (counter) — Think. You've heard the objection; here is why it fails.
  • C (conclusion) — Act. The call that lands.

Name the Effect you are pursuing at every beat. Plans that only chase Think (logic) lose AO5 marks for failing to move the reader.

Banned patterns — they cost AO5 and AO6 marks

  • Shows / showing / shown anywhere in the piece. Replace with illustrates, demonstrates, emphasises, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, signals, enacts.
  • Abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to). Replace with a concrete image.
  • More than ten to be verbs across the piece (is / are / was / were). Use active verbs.
  • More than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.
  • Discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly … Secondly … Finally). Bridge with verbal connectors instead.
  • Triadic structures without parallel grammar.
  • Vague intensifiers (really, very, just, basically).
  • American register (move as a noun, gotten, trash). British English only.

Voice rules — "use my voice confidently"

  • Direct address (you, we, us) creates solidarity but loses force if overused. Anchor it in I, V, and the final C.
  • One personal anecdote, no more, sits inside M. A second anecdote flattens the piece.
  • Rhetorical question cap = three across the whole piece. One in I; one in U or V; an optional third in the Conclusion.
  • Call to action sits in the Conclusion. Make it specific (write to your MP, switch the bedroom for downstairs), not vague (make a change).
  • Audience register is judged on AO5. A speech to peers ≠ a letter to a Minister. Match it.

Q1 — Social media and smartphone harm to teenagers

Q1 — Social media and smartphone harm to teenagers

Why this is likely. AQA Q5 has never directly tested social-media or smartphone harm to teenagers. It is the most overdue cluster on the statement-of-opinion frame. Current-affairs salience is high — the Smartphone Free Childhood movement, the Online Safety Act 2023, Jonathan Haidt's The Anxious Generation — and AQA's 19th-century paired-source archive includes Victorian anxieties about the telegraph, the gramophone, and the sensationalist press, all of which pair cleanly with a 21st-century smartphone-harm article.

Three plausible stems (AQA prints ONE of these shapes):

  1. "Smartphones are stealing childhood. Schools should ban them; parents should hide them; teenagers should learn to live without them." Write an article for a broadsheet newspaper in which you argue your point of view.
  2. "Social media is not the problem. The problem is parents who never set limits and schools that never modelled focus." Write a letter to the Minister for Education explaining your point of view.
  3. "Some adults complain that teenagers spend too much time online. They forget that the screen is a window, not a prison." Write a speech for your school Leavers' Day responding to this statement.

Frame. Smartphones did not invent teenage loneliness; they industrialised it. The Sophicly position is not anti-technology — it is anti-design-without-conscience. Every algorithm engineered for maximum attention is engineered against the developing teenage brain. The reform target is not the user; it is the design.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Effect: Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Sensory snapshot: "Half-past midnight. The bedroom is dark; the face is lit blue. The thumb keeps scrolling, because the thumb has forgotten the brain it belongs to."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "Ofcom found that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life."
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture every fifteen-year-old in Britain handing over their phone for one night. Picture the silence."
  • Topic bridge: name the controlling concept in one line — design that profits from attention is design that costs childhood.
  • Tone: urgent, scholarly, never pleading.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Effect: Feel)

  • Opening image: the attention economy as a casino built for children.
  • Metaphor: "Every notification is a slot-machine handle pulled in a child's pocket, and every win is engineered to lose."
  • Extension: trace the metaphor — small dopamine wins, escalating spend, the family table emptying of attention.
  • Evidence: Ofcom 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes report (online-time data for 12–17 year-olds); one image of a family meal where every face is downturned.
  • Devices: triadic structure on the casino metaphor (pull / win / lose); short-sentence pivot ("This is not entertainment.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Effect: Think)

  • M1 — Design, not user. Frame: regulation must target the engineers, not the children. Metaphor: "We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards." Develop: name the design features that hook (infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks). Evidence: one statistic on platform retention algorithms.
  • M2 — School as sanctuary. Frame: classrooms protected from device intrusion outperform classrooms that surrender. Metaphor: "A school without phones is a school where attention can breathe again." Develop: phone-free pilot schools; reduced lunch-hall isolation; restored eye contact. Evidence: a named UK phone-free trial (Smartphone Free Childhood, 2024).
  • M3 — Parents as architects. Frame: parents are not the police; they are the architects of the home's attention. Metaphor: "Charging stations downstairs are not punishment — they are scaffolding." Develop: the household rule that the bedroom is for sleep, not for the algorithm. Evidence: one specific household routine that works.
  • Transitions — verbal bridges only: The design is one half. The classroom is the second. The home is the third. Never Firstly / Secondly / Finally.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Effect: Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The bedside table holds books."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Family meals stretch into conversations the household had almost forgotten possible."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Childhood breathes again."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Effect: Think) — three options

  • Connection objection: "Smartphones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline."
  • Punishment objection: "Banning phones in schools punishes the responsible majority for the irresponsible few."
  • Trust objection: "Parents who confiscate phones lose the trust they need most."
  • Refutation pivot (works for all three): "There is connection on the platforms — and there is engineered loneliness around it. Both are real. Only one is profitable."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Effect: Act)

  • Circular echo: return to the bedroom-at-midnight image; the blue light; the thumb; the brain.
  • New move: the thumb stops. The phone goes downstairs. The face turns to the dark, and the dark holds rest, not the algorithm.
  • Final sentence: "Childhood is not a market. Stop selling it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a broadsheet newspaper, ~720 words) — opens with a thinking-aloud taxonomy hook

[I — Introduction]

There are two types of attention. There is the kind you give — to a friend, a book, a conversation. And there is the kind that is taken from you, quietly, by something built to take it. A fourteen-year-old in your house has been handing over the second kind since she was eleven, and a Californian boardroom has been logging it at three in the morning while her body slept. Think of it like money: type one is what you earn; type two is what someone else earns from you. Except nothing here is earned. It is engineered. The notification, the infinite scroll, the streak — each is a small machine built to convert a child's attention into a company's revenue, and it works.

[U — Urgency]

The numbers are not subtle. Ofcom's 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes report — the most authoritative UK dataset on this — finds that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online, which is almost half their waking life outside school. Imagine a Victorian school inspector being told that the average pupil now devotes half her free hours to a piece of glass an industry has paid to make addictive. He would have written it down in his ledger. He would have looked up. He would not have written anything else for a while.

[M1 — Design, not user]

The instinct, when something goes wrong with a child, is to regulate the child. The instinct is wrong here. We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards. The reform target — once you have noticed what type of attention is being extracted, and by whom — is not the fourteen-year-old at midnight. It is the boardroom in Menlo Park that decided, in the morning, that her sleep was a market. Infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks, the gentle dopamine architecture of a hundred small wins ordered to mask a single larger loss: each of these features was built. None of them is an accident.

[M2 — School as sanctuary]

There is a third kind of attention worth naming. Call it sanctuary-attention: the kind a classroom holds when the phones are not in it. The Smartphone Free Childhood movement, started by two British mothers in 2024, has signed parental pacts in tens of thousands of UK households now, and the schools that have followed report the same quiet pattern: lunch halls talking again, corridors remembering eye contact, lessons holding for forty minutes instead of forty seconds. Phones are not banned. They are simply asked to wait outside the room where the learning lives.

[M3 — Parents as architects]

And there is a fourth kind: domestic-attention, the kind a household decides to keep. Parents are not the police; they are the architects of the home's attention. A charging station downstairs is not punishment; it is scaffolding. The bedroom is for sleep, not for the algorithm. One household rule, applied without apology, does what a thousand well-meaning lectures cannot.

[V — Vision]

The bedside table holds books. Family meals stretch into conversations the household had almost forgotten possible. Childhood breathes again.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say, in good faith, that smartphones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline. That is true. There is connection on the platforms. There is also engineered loneliness around it. Both are real. Only one is profitable. The teenager who finds her community online has, somewhere underneath that finding, been first made lonely by the very architecture that now offers her the cure.

[C — Conclusion]

So. Two types of attention. The kind we give, and the kind that is taken. We have been doing the second by accident for fifteen years. The proposal is that we now, for the first time, do the first on purpose. Phone downstairs. Bedroom dark. Childhood not for sale.

Alternative stems. The same IUMVCC shell answers Stem 2 (letter to the Minister for Education on parents and schools modelling focus) and Stem 3 (speech for Leavers' Day on screens as windows not prisons). The thinking-aloud taxonomy ("two types of…") works cleanly for an article register; for the letter or the speech, pivot to a measured-definition opening or a repetition hook — pick whatever fits the audience.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Casino metaphor extended I → U → Conclusion. Three rhetorical-question cap respected. No shows. No abstract concept nouns. Direct address anchored to I, V, Conclusion only.

Q2 — Environment, climate action, individual responsibility

Q2 — Environment, climate action, individual responsibility

Why this is likely. AQA Q5 has touched the environmental cluster from the side — Nov 2021 (fast fashion), Jun 2020 (travel and damage) — but never directly asked about individual climate action. The cluster pairs with AQA's 19th-century archive of natural-history writing (Victorian Alpine letters, Hawaiian travel, London Zoo) and 21st-century climate journalism. The 2026 candidate sits inside a decade of record-breaking UK temperatures.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have run out of someone-else-must-act. Every household, every postcode, every weekday morning is now where the climate is decided." Write a speech for a community meeting.
  2. "Personal recycling, electric cars and meat-free Mondays will not save the planet. The problem is corporate, not individual." Write an article for a magazine arguing for or against.
  3. "It is governments, not citizens, who failed the climate. Stop guilting the household." Write a letter to a national newspaper.

Frame. Individual climate action without policy is performance, but policy without citizen pressure is decoration. The Sophicly position holds both at once: the household votes with its choices, but the choice that matters most is the vote at the ballot box.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Sensory snapshot: "Forty degrees in July. The tarmac softens. The radio reads a record temperature and the country shrugs, then drives to the beach."
  • Bold statement: "There is no household innocent of the climate."
  • Imagine scenario: "Picture 2050. The summer that was once a record is now a Tuesday."
  • Topic bridge: the climate is not a crisis to feel; it is a posture to take.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: climate change as a slow flood already inside the home.
  • Metaphor: "The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway."
  • Extension: the carpet damp; the wallpaper bubbling; the family pretending nothing.
  • Evidence: Met Office UK record-temperature counts since 2018, or Carbon Brief data on UK emissions falling while consumption emissions rise.
  • Devices: triadic structure (carpet / wallpaper / family); short pivot ("This is the room.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The household. Frame: the smallest unit of climate policy is the kitchen. Metaphor: "Your fridge is a small parliament." Develop: the meat-free week, the thermostat at nineteen, the boycotted brand. Evidence: one fact on diet-related emissions.
  • M2 — The street. Frame: street-level pressure changes councils. Metaphor: "Pavements are policy waiting to happen." Develop: tree-planting petitions, school-streets schemes, community insulation co-operatives. Evidence: a named UK street-level scheme.
  • M3 — The ballot box. Frame: the vote is the loudest action a household can take. Metaphor: "A ballot is the household's longest lever." Develop: voting on local council climate plans; pressuring MPs on the 2050 net-zero pathway. Evidence: the 2024 Climate Change Committee progress report.
  • Transitions: The kitchen is one step. The street is the next. The ballot is the last.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The street has trees again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Britain breathes."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Corporate-only objection: "Individual action is a distraction; only corporate regulation matters."
  • Wealth objection: "Wealthier households can afford solar panels and electric cars; the poorest cannot, so the policy is unfair."
  • Despair objection: "Britain is two percent of global emissions; nothing we do moves the needle."
  • Refutation pivot: "The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore. Withdraw the consumer and the corporation moves. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. Both move together, or neither moves."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the forty-degree July returns; the radio now reads a falling number.
  • New move: the household has chosen; the street has chosen; the ballot has chosen.
  • Final sentence: "The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech for a community meeting, ~720 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

The climate is not a crisis; it is a machine — a vast, intricate, geophysical apparatus of currents and feedbacks and slow-moving forces, an interlocking architecture of ocean conveyors and stratospheric jet streams and the warm Atlantic drift that keeps this country green in February, all of it pressed by industry and exhaust and the small daily theft of the household commute. Every fraction of a degree we have added to the average temperature is, behind the headline, the result of a machine running hot. The Met Office confirmed in 2022 that the United Kingdom had passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history. Behind that headline sat the apparatus. The machine has only one possible end-state if we keep feeding it.

[U — Urgency]

This is not slow. The 2024 Climate Change Committee progress report told Parliament the United Kingdom is no longer on track to meet its 2030 emissions targets — that the existing policy mix, the existing pace of decarbonisation, the existing schedule of renewables deployment, and the existing structure of the domestic transport fleet will not, on their current trajectories, hit the legally binding target the country wrote into statute. Read that twice. The country has set its own target and is preparing to miss it. The Carbon Brief data on consumption emissions confirm what the policy data implies: we are exporting our pollution faster than we are eliminating it.

[M1 — The household]

And yet. The kitchen — that small, low-power, daily-running engine — is also a machine, and a household-scale machine is the unit at which the country is built. Three meals a day, multiplied by sixty-five million households, multiplied by 365 days a year, produces the supply chain the nation drives. A meat-free week shifts measurable carbon out of that chain; a thermostat at nineteen, not twenty-two, lets the gas-fired power station breathe out a little less; a second-hand coat worn for a winter, not the new one ordered overnight, costs less and survives further. The kitchen, multiplied, is policy in disguise.

[M2 — The street]

Pavements are policy waiting to happen. The school-streets schemes in Hackney, the community insulation co-operatives in South Wales, the residents' tree-planting petitions that filled council estates in Manchester with cherry trees last spring — these projects looked, in their early weeks, like noise. They became, in their later months, the infrastructure the council was forced to recognise. Street pressure is the country's most underestimated political device.

[M3 — The ballot box]

The ballot is the household's longest lever — and the only mechanism that can shift the country back onto the 2030 target the Climate Change Committee has measured us against. The kitchen and the street do their work; the ballot box does the rest. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore.

[V — Vision]

The street has trees again. Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off. Buses run on the timetable they were promised; insulation has stilled the draught beneath every front door; the high street has remembered the cobbler. Britain breathes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say individual action is a distraction; only corporate regulation matters. There is truth in this. There is also the harder truth: the corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore, and the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Pull the consumer and the corporation moves; pull the voter and the regulator wakes. Both move together, or neither moves.

[C — Conclusion]

The machine runs hot. We can throttle it back from the kitchen, from the street, from the ballot box, from a thousand small simultaneous interventions repeated by sixty-five million households every Tuesday morning until the curve bends. The science is patient. The decade is not. The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article disputing "personal action will not save the planet — only corporate regulation will") and Stem 3 (letter to a national newspaper rejecting the household-blame frame). The cascading extended-metaphor opening suits the speech form; for the letter, tighten the long sentences into a measured-definition opening instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Flood-in-the-hallway metaphor anchored I → U → V → Conclusion. Three rhetorical-question cap respected. No shows. Triadic structures parallel.

Q3 — Animal welfare, zoos, captivity, pets

Q3 — Animal welfare, zoos, captivity, pets

Why this is likely. Last visited at AQA Q5 in November 2019 with the captivity prompt ("All animals should be free!"). Five-year recurrence pattern. AQA's archive is rich in 19th-century natural-history writing (Bartlett at London Zoo, 1898; Hudson in Patagonia, 1893) and 21st-century wildlife journalism (Born Free Foundation reports; rewilding stories).

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Zoos are nineteenth-century institutions pretending to be twenty-first-century arks. Shut them." Write an article for a magazine.
  2. "Pet ownership in modern flats is cruelty dressed as companionship." Write a letter to a national newspaper.
  3. "Conservation needs zoos. The alternative is a generation of children who never see what we are losing." Write a speech for a school assembly.

Frame. The question is not whether we love animals. We do. The question is whether the institutions we built in their name have outgrown the morality that built them.

I — INTRODUCTION (50–100 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Sensory snapshot: "A polar bear paces three steps left, three steps right, three steps left. The glass between you is the only thing it cannot break."
  • Counterintuitive fact: "More tigers are held in captivity in the United States alone than survive in the wild across the whole of Asia."
  • Rhetorical question: "What is a zoo for, if the animal inside it has lost the instincts of its own species?"
  • Topic bridge: the institution outlived the ethics.

U — URGENCY (100–150 words · Feel)

  • Image: the cage as a museum exhibit of our slow conscience.
  • Metaphor: "Every enclosure is a Victorian souvenir we have forgotten how to throw away."
  • Extension: the keepers know it; the children sense it; the species pace it.
  • Evidence: captive-animal stereotypy figures from Born Free Foundation; wild population collapse statistic from WWF Living Planet Report.
  • Devices: triadic (keepers / children / species); short pivot ("This is not education.").

M — METHODOLOGY (250–350 words · Think)

  • M1 — The pacing. Frame: stereotypic behaviour is the body of the captive animal telling the truth our mouths will not. Metaphor: "The pacing is the lecture." Develop: behavioural studies on captive primate distress; the elephant that no longer knows how to socialise. Evidence: BIAZA welfare reports or Born Free Foundation data.
  • M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle. Frame: the sanctuary feeds the animal first; the zoo sells the ticket first. Metaphor: "One is a hospital. The other is a theatre." Develop: named UK sanctuaries (Big Cat Sanctuary, Wildlife Heritage Foundation). Contrast with the safari-park business model. Evidence: revenue breakdown — welfare versus turnstile.
  • M3 — Conservation in the wild. Frame: real conservation is field-based, slow, and rarely glamorous. Metaphor: "A reserve is a library. A zoo is a postcard." Develop: rewilding projects (Knepp, Yellowstone); in-situ breeding programmes funded without enclosures. Evidence: a named rewilding metric.
  • Transitions: The body speaks. The institutions choose. The field corrects.

V — VISION (100–150 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The enclosure stands empty."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The funding has moved to the field where the animal still belongs."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Conscience caught up."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Education objection: "Children need to see real animals; otherwise conservation has no public."
  • Funding objection: "Zoos fund the conservation work nobody else pays for."
  • Endangered-species objection: "Some species now exist only in captivity; close the zoos and they vanish."
  • Refutation pivot: "Children seeing a captive tiger learn that captivity is acceptable. The lesson teaches the cage, not the species."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the polar bear's three steps left, three steps right.
  • New move: today, the keeper unlocks; the bear walks; the steps lengthen.
  • Final sentence: "Cages are an apology we are still owed. Pay it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~730 words) — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

You have heard about our greatest acts of conservation. You have heard them every time a primary school party files past the orangutan house at London Zoo, every time a documentary opens with the words this rare species can now be seen safely behind glass, every time a charity advert reminds you that without zoos the tiger would already be gone. You have heard, in short, that zoos save species. That zoos exist for the species. That the institution and the conservation share the same purpose.

Except that's a complete lie. The zoo and the conservation share a single word, and almost nothing else. Real conservation lives in the field. Captive display lives behind glass. To confuse the two is to mistake the postcard for the country it depicts.

[U — Urgency]

Every enclosure is a Victorian souvenir we have forgotten how to throw away. The keepers know it. The children sense it. The species pace it. The Born Free Foundation has documented stereotypic behaviours — the pacing, the head-bobbing, the obsessive grooming — in captive animals across British zoos for over thirty years, and the WWF Living Planet Report (2024) records a sixty-nine percent average decline in wildlife populations globally since 1970. Outside the cage, the species is collapsing. Inside the cage, the species is acting out the collapse. This is not education. This is exhibition.

[M1 — The pacing]

The pacing is the lecture. Stereotypic behaviour is the body of the captive animal telling the truth our mouths will not — that the enclosure designed for our viewing is the enclosure designed against the animal's sanity. BIAZA's welfare standards have grown more sophisticated each decade, and the better zoos have shrunk their elephant herds, retired their dolphins, and dismantled their cetacean tanks. But sophistication is not absolution. An enclosure with enrichment is still an enclosure, and a tiger that learns to hunt frozen-mince balls is still a tiger that has been redesigned for the gift shop.

[M2 — Sanctuary, not spectacle]

One is a hospital. The other is a theatre. The Big Cat Sanctuary in Kent, the Wildlife Heritage Foundation, and the Born Free–rescue partnerships in Ethiopia and South Africa exist to feed the animal first and sell the ticket last — and they do not pretend the visit is the point. The safari park, by contrast, was designed around the family ticket: the lion sleeps where the postcard can be taken, and the gift shop is open from nine. The business models are not equivalent. They are opposites with the same word painted on the door.

[M3 — Conservation in the wild]

A reserve is a library. A zoo is a postcard. Real conservation is field-based, slow, and rarely glamorous: the Knepp rewilding estate in West Sussex, the wolf reintroduction at Yellowstone, the in-situ breeding programmes in Madagascar and Borneo that nobody buys a ticket to attend. Each of these costs less per protected species than maintaining a captive equivalent, and each of these returns the animal to the system the animal evolved inside. The cage cannot do that. The cage has never done that.

[V — Vision]

The enclosure stands empty. The funding has moved to the field where the animal still belongs. Conscience caught up.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say children need to see real animals; otherwise conservation has no public. There is something in that. There is also this: children seeing a captive tiger learn that captivity is acceptable. The lesson teaches the cage, not the species. The David Attenborough documentary, the school-supported in-situ trip, the high-definition livestream from a real reserve — each does what the zoo claims to do, without the body of an animal being asked to perform.

[C — Conclusion]

So believe, by all means, that zoos and conservation share a word. Believe that the brochures will tell you they share a mission. Then look at the polar bear pacing three steps left, three steps right, three steps left. Believe whichever of the two the bear is telling you. Cages are an apology we are still owed. Pay it.

Alternative stems. The same shell inverts for Stem 3 (speech to a school assembly arguing FOR zoos as conservation): the M-beats reframe field-conservation as PARTNERED with — not replacing — visitor-facing zoos, and the counter-argument becomes the Stem 1 position. The assumption-then-reversal hook works in either direction; the assumption you set up at the open is the one you destroy at the close.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Captivity-as-Victorian-museum metaphor extended I → U → M → V → Conclusion. No shows. No abstract concept nouns.

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the 7:30am kitchen returns; the fifteen-year-old now leaves at 9:15.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (letter to the Minister for Education, ~720 words) — opens with a measured-definition hook

[I — Introduction]

Dear Minister,

A school day is, before anything else, a calendar arrangement. It is the hours we have chosen to put between the moment a child wakes and the moment we ask her to learn. In Britain, we have chosen those hours by inheritance — by the discipline of the Victorian factory, the postwar shift pattern, the unspoken assumption that childhood should rehearse the working day before it arrives at one. These were not pedagogical decisions. They were industrial ones. They have outlasted, by a clean century, the economy that produced them.

[U — Urgency]

We pull adolescents out of bed and call the daze 'discipline'. The NHS and the Sleep Foundation both recommend between eight and ten hours of sleep a night for thirteen-to-eighteen-year-olds; the British school day begins on the assumption that they need six. The maths lesson at 8:55 that nobody remembers by 10:30 is not a failure of teaching. The playground stumble at 8:40 is not a failure of resilience. The science exam at 9:00 that decides a future at 9:48 is not a fair test. This is not teaching. This is a calendar pitched against a body.

[M1 — The science]

A teenage circadian clock is not a broken adult clock; it is a different machine. Crowley et al., writing in Current Biology in 2019, confirmed that adolescent melatonin shifts the body's natural sleep window one to three hours later than an adult's, and that this delay is biological — not behavioural, not lazy, not a smartphone problem. We have known this for two decades. We continue to time the country's school day as if we had not learned it.

[M2 — The hours]

A 10am start is not luxury; it is alignment. The University of Oxford's 2019 study of UK schools that shifted their start times reported significant improvements in attendance and academic attainment, replicating Kyla Wahlstrom's longitudinal findings from US districts that moved their high-school day from a 7:20am to an 8:30am start. The same children, in the same classroom, with the same teachers, performed better simply because their bodies had been allowed to wake. Ninety minutes of clock-shift bought the country a measurably different generation. Ninety minutes is a policy. It is not a miracle.

[M3 — The exam]

The exam is not the problem; the volume is. Forty exams in five years is not assessment; it is attrition. The 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report, published in March of this year, has finally said in public what teachers have said in private for a decade: that the breadth of GCSE testing is dilution rather than rigour, and that the same standards can be sampled at the end of school without the year-on-year wear. Reduce the volume. Keep the rigour. The two are not the same.

[V — Vision]

Lessons begin at ten. Maths classrooms catch a brain that has had time to wake. The exam timetable holds the same standard inside a calendar half the size. Learning fits the body.

[C — Counter-argument]

I anticipate the objection that a late start would inconvenience working parents. It would, briefly. Working parents already inconvenience themselves around an alarm pitched against their children. The inconvenience exists; we are merely choosing whose body bears it. A staggered start, a flexible morning, a school day that ends at five rather than three — every working parent who has heard that proposal has, in my experience, asked for the school to lead. They are waiting for the policy. Lead it.

[C — Conclusion]

Sir, the change is small. Ninety minutes on a clock face. One legislative consultation. One implementation cycle. A generation that arrives at the classroom awake, learns inside a calendar that was built for it, and carries the school day rather than survives it. Move the bell. The science is patient. The decade is not.

Yours sincerely,

[a parent / a teacher / a sixth-form student — depending on the writer]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article defending GCSEs) by inverting M3 — the volume becomes the discipline the country needs — and Stem 3 (speech to parent governors on a 10am start). The measured-definition opening works for any of the three; what changes is the register, not the architecture.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Different-machine metaphor anchored I → M1 → Conclusion. No shows. No discourse markers.

Q5 — Sport: commercialisation, integrity, fairness

  • Circular echo: the home crowd, the away crowd, the pitch.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a national newspaper, ~720 words) — opens with a concrete-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Think about this: a Premier League striker in 2025 earns, in a single Saturday afternoon, more than the volunteer coach of his old under-eight team will earn across the next seven years. The salaries are not from different industries. They are from the same one. They are priced by the same broadcast rights, paid for by the same advertising revenue, funded by the same television-watching public — and yet the distance between the highest-paid player and the parent who runs Saturday-morning training has reached, in real terms, the distance between two entirely different economies. Football has stopped pretending. It is no longer a sport; it is an entertainment industry that wears the badge of one.

[U — Urgency]

Think about this too: while Premier League average ticket prices have more than tripled in real terms since 1989, Sport England's 2024 Active Lives: Children and Young People Survey records that thirty-six percent of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet recommended activity levels. The top of the pyramid has never been wealthier. The base of the pyramid has never been thinner. FIFPRO reported in 2023 that elite players now compete in up to seventy matches a season — a twenty-percent increase over a decade — and in the same year, parish-council records across England logged the highest annual number of recreational-pitch closures since the figure began being tracked.

I was watching, last week, a televised Saturday-night match in a stadium I could never afford a seat in. I was thinking, as I watched, when was the last time the youngest child in this city played on a free council pitch. Probably never. I thought, this is their pitch.

[M1 — The wage]

When a player earns in a week what a fan earns in a decade, the bond is broken. A salary that long is a wall that long. The reform here is not envy; it is gravity. A structural wage cap — applied across the league, transparently published, levied to fund grassroots — would not flatten the talent; it would re-anchor the sport. Sport England's Active Lives survey records that thirty-six percent of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet recommended activity levels, and the closure of school and council pitches is one of the most cited reasons. Cap the top; rebuild the bottom. The same money does both.

[M2 — The fixture]

We are wringing a sponge that has nothing left to give. The expanded Champions League — thirty-six teams, more match-days, more flights — and the inaugural Club World Cup did not enrich football; they exhausted it. The player-burnout interviews of 2024, given by some of the game's most senior names, named the schedule as the injury. FIFPRO's 2023 workload research recorded a measurable rise in soft-tissue injuries among players asked to play across four competitions in one season. The sport is selling more games and producing fewer of them at full capacity. This is not commerce. This is asset-stripping.

[M3 — The next generation]

A river does not run if you concrete the source. Protect grassroots, and the elite game self-corrects. School-pitch divestment, council-pitch closures, the thirty-pound fee that ends a child's Saturday football — each of these has a price the country is paying twenty years later. Sport England's 2024 data show that the participation gap between richer and poorer postcodes is now widening, not narrowing, and the FA's own grassroots-investment shortfall has been openly debated in Parliament. The Premier League funds the grassroots. The grassroots funds the Premier League. Cut the second, and the first runs dry.

[V — Vision]

Saturday afternoons return. Boys and girls fill the pitch their grandparents walked off. Sport remembers itself.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say capping wages drives the best players to other leagues. The best players already follow the cap that exists in every league outside Britain. Quality is not what flees; obscene rents are what flee. Let them. A league that proves the game can survive without its highest salaries proves something far more valuable: that the sport, not the billboard, was the asset all along.

[C — Conclusion]

Think about this last time: the striker earns a million pounds a week. The under-eight coach earns nothing. The striker plays in front of seventy thousand. The coach trains eight children, each of whose parents work shifts to keep the household running. The striker is celebrated as the country's poet. The coach is the country's poet. We have invested the wage, the broadcast and the billboard in only one of them. Sport is the country's longest poem. Stop printing it on a billboard.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (speech for a school sports awards ceremony on women's-sport pay) — substitute the women's-game economics for the wage / fixture / grassroots triad — and Stem 3 (letter to a sports council on PEDs as a system-reward problem). The concrete-contrast hook works in all three; pick the two unequal images and let them argue against each other.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a broadsheet newspaper, ~730 words) — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

You have heard about the AI essay crisis. You have heard it from the secondary-school teacher who marked thirty pieces of coursework last term and could no longer tell which sixteen-year-old had written which. You have heard it from the university lecturer who has stopped setting take-home essays altogether. You have heard it from the parent who suspects, correctly, that the homework completed in eight minutes was not completed by their fifteen-year-old. You have heard, in short, that AI is the death of writing.

Except that's a complete lie. AI is not the death of writing. AI is the death of fake writing — the death of the kind of essay that summarised when it should have argued, that recalled when it should have wrestled, that paraphrased when it should have thought. The model can outsource summary, paraphrase and recall, because they were never the point. The crisis is not the tool. The crisis is what the tool reveals about how thinly we have been teaching thought.

[U — Urgency]

The factory used to be in a Bangalore office. Now it is in the bedroom. JISC's 2024 Generative AI: student and staff survey of over seven thousand UK students found more than half had used generative AI for academic work, and detection remained inconsistent across institutions. The marking has changed. The moderation has changed. The meaning of a grade is changing in real time. This is not learning. This is the silent transfer of the mind's work to a machine that did not need to learn anything to perform it.

[M1 — The classroom]

Bring writing back into the room. An essay drafted on paper, in pen, in front of a teacher is an essay the machine cannot ghost. The oral viva, used in the Cambridge English Faculty for decades, is one of the oldest defences against intellectual outsourcing — and the most modest. A fifteen-minute conversation with a sixteen-year-old about the essay they have just submitted will tell any teacher, in any subject, whether the words on the page were the words inside the head. We do not need new technology to detect AI essays. We need an old practice we stopped using.

[M2 — The assessment]

Stop asking students to summarise; ask them to argue. The 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report, published by the Department for Education in March of this year, reframed the question entirely: the issue is not whether AI will write the answer; the issue is whether the answer was worth asking. Questions that reward the wrestle — "what do you make of this?", "where is this argument vulnerable?", "what would the other side write?" — do not have a single retrievable answer the model can hand back. They have a thinking, which is the thing the model does not yet do.

[M3 — The literacy]

Teach the model as a tool, not a ghost. Teach the calculator and the child still learns the maths. Finland's national curriculum framework, updated in 2023 by the Finnish National Agency for Education, treats digital and AI literacy as a cross-curricular competency — not a separate subject, not a feared intrusion, but a literacy to be developed alongside reading and writing. Pupils learn what the model is, what it can and cannot do, how to disclose its use, and how to recognise its limits. Banning the machine produces secret use. Teaching the machine produces honest use. Choose the second.

[V — Vision]

Pens return to the desk. Sixteen-year-olds argue out loud the paragraphs they will write. The oral viva returns. Thinking is taught.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say banning AI in schools is Luddism — the workplace already uses it. The workplace uses calculators too; schools still teach long division. The point of school is not to mimic the workplace; it is to build the mind the workplace will, eventually, employ. A graduate who can prompt an AI without thinking is a graduate the AI does not need to employ.

[C — Conclusion]

So believe, if you must, that AI killed the essay. Look at the essays it killed. They were not essays. They were paragraphs of paraphrase wearing the costume of argument. The model exposed them, and the exposure is the opportunity we have been handed: to teach writing as thinking again, to make every essay a wrestle, to make every grade a defence of an idea. The pen returns to the desk. The viva returns to the classroom. Writing is the country's last private classroom. Defend it.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a magazine, ~720 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

Tourism in 2026 is a machine of staggering scale and untracked appetite — a billion-traveller engine of cruise ships and short-haul jets and platform-rented apartments and migrating itineraries and the slow, daily consumption of cities by the visitors who came to admire them, a system of arrivals and departures that has, in a single generation, scaled past every regulatory mechanism we built to contain it, and has now reached the point at which the destinations it celebrates are being measurably worn away by the celebration itself. Santorini, in the summer of 2025, hosted three thousand passengers from a single cruise ship onto an island built for eight hundred. That is the machine. That is the appetite.

[U — Urgency]

The Eiffel Tower waits in a queue that begins in another country. Rents in central Lisbon, Amsterdam, Athens and Barcelona have been priced out of locals' reach by short-stay platforms; restaurant menus are printed in English; the bakery that used to be a bakery is now a souvenir shop. The UN World Tourism Organization recorded over 1.4 billion international tourist arrivals in 2023 — a return to pre-pandemic peaks at a moment when several of the world's most-visited cities had already declared themselves overwhelmed. This is not travel. This is the slow consumption of place by the people who came to admire it.

[M1 — The traveller]

A holiday is not a trophy. Travel slower, travel fewer times, travel closer. Eurostar's 2024 demand data show passenger numbers on the London–Paris route at record levels, with carbon footprint per passenger a fraction of the equivalent flight. One big trip a decade, planned for, anticipated and remembered, will give a household more than five short-haul trips a year — and the difference is not just carbon. It is also the trip itself, restored to the thing it was meant to be: an event, not an entry on a calendar.

[M2 — The destination]

Cities like Venice need a turnstile, not a tide. Amsterdam's 2023–24 city-council policy banned new hotels in the centre, capped cruise-ship arrivals, and redirected tourist-tax revenue into housing for residents. Venice itself, in 2024, became the first major city to introduce a paid day-tripper ticket. These are not anti-tourism measures. They are pro-place measures. They protect the city the visitor came to see by ensuring the city is still recognisably itself by the time the visitor arrives.

[M3 — The industry]

The cruise ship is the unit of damage. A floating town that pays no taxes and leaves no money. Transport and Environment's 2024 cruise-emissions research recorded that the largest ships emit more sulphur in a single day than millions of cars combined, and that the coastal towns hosting them — Dubrovnik, Kotor, Santorini — see a measurable per-visitor decline in retained tourism spend the larger the ship gets. The big ship is the visible part. The under-taxed regulatory loophole that lets it dock is the part that has to change.

[V — Vision]

The street is quiet on August Tuesdays. Locals walk to bakeries that still bake; rents fall back to what wages can carry. The island remembers itself. Place outlasts visit.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say tourism is the livelihood of millions in developing economies — and it is. It is exactly why it should not be left to collapse under its own weight. Cap the volume, raise the per-visitor levy, redistribute the proceeds to the housing the local workforce can no longer afford, and the livelihood is preserved by the very policies that look, on the surface, like restrictions. The alternative is the destination's quiet death — and the livelihood dies with it.

[C — Conclusion]

The machine runs. The destinations bear it. The destinations cannot bear it indefinitely. Cap the volume, raise the levy, redirect the proceeds to the local rent the visitor's arrival has been quietly raising — and the engine throttles back to the rate at which the destination can still recognise itself. Travel less. Travel deeper. Pay the place its rent.

Alternative stems. The cascading extended-metaphor opening fits the article and the speech; the letter benefits from a measured-definition open instead.

Model answer — written to Stem 3 (speech for a Year 11 careers evening, ~720 words) — opens with a thinking-aloud taxonomy hook

[I — Introduction]

Good evening, Year Eleven. There are two types of jobs. Useful ones, and lucrative ones. They ought to be the same jobs. They are not.

Useful jobs include teaching, nursing, midwifery, the ambulance driver, the bin collector, the GP-surgery receptionist who keeps the whole place running on a salary nobody envies, the school librarian who made sure you read enough by sixteen to one day write a speech like this one. Lucrative jobs include management consultancy, derivatives trading, the cosmetic clinic that markets to teenagers, and whatever it is the people who own football clubs do all day. The two lists barely overlap. The pay packets do not even pretend to.

[U — Urgency]

We are picking the fruit and forgetting to water the tree. Across this country, the GP surgery cannot recruit. The new-build school is short five teachers in September. The bus you took here may have been cancelled at six this morning because the depot had no driver. Department for Education and NHS England vacancy data from 2024 confirm what every household in Britain already suspects: the work that holds this country together is the work this country has quietly stopped paying. This is not a labour market. This is an emptying.

[M1 — The pay]

A newly qualified secondary teacher in 2025 earns less, in real terms, than they did in 2010. Institute for Fiscal Studies analysis confirms the figure across teaching, nursing, and the wider public-sector workforce: real-terms pay has eroded across more than a decade, and the country is now paying the predictable price — a vacancy rate that hollows out the very services we pretend to admire. Nursing is national infrastructure; we should pay it like infrastructure. Teaching is national infrastructure; we should pay it like infrastructure. The Treasury that cannot afford to fund a classroom this year will, before the decade is out, be paying for the classroom that never opened.

[M2 — The story]

Schools tell teenagers the wrong heroes. Career assemblies in this country still look more like the Times Rich List than the local hospital. Sutton Trust research on careers aspirations has shown for years that teenagers from lower-income backgrounds receive less exposure to professional careers, and almost no exposure to vocational careers presented as aspirations rather than fall-backs. Tonight's evening is a small correction; the national story is a longer one. Pair every careers event with named local vocational figures. Rebalance the prestige curriculum. The country your school describes is the country your school produces.

[M3 — The route]

A degree is not the only road. Apprenticeships are not a lower drawer; they are a different cabinet. Department for Education and Skills England apprenticeship-outcomes data — published since IFATE was dissolved into Skills England in April 2024 — show degree-equivalent apprenticeships in engineering, healthcare, and construction now match or exceed graduate-route earnings within five years. The country needs the routes. The routes pay for the country. Dismantle the parental snobbery that is still telling capable sixteen-year-olds that the apprenticeship is the second-best decision.

[V — Vision]

Wards fill again. Sixth-formers choose teaching because the work means something, and the paycheque admits it. Engineering desks fill. Construction sites take their own apprentices. Purpose is paid.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say higher pay for nurses and teachers means higher taxes for everyone. It means a tax on the country to maintain the country. The alternative — a country that cannot staff its own classrooms and hospitals — is a far more expensive bill, paid in a currency no Chancellor wants to print. The bill arrives either way. The only question is whether the country pays it in salary, or pays it in collapse.

[C — Conclusion]

So. Two types of jobs. Useful ones. Lucrative ones. The country needs them to be the same job. The country has, for forty years now, been pretending they are different. Tonight, in this hall, I am going to ask Year Eleven to refuse the pretence. Tomorrow, the country will hand you a labour market. Choose the work that holds the country, and the country will, eventually, hold the work. Choose purpose. The pay packet will follow — or the country will collapse before it does.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 1 (article for a school magazine on the funnelling of brightest students) and Stem 2 (letter to the Minister for Health on nursing pay). Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a teenage magazine, ~720 words) — opens with a historical-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Think about this: a Victorian fourteen-year-old, in 1880, looked at herself in a dressing-table mirror that her mother had bought. The mirror told her, more or less, the truth — slightly flattering at the angle her grandmother had chosen for the room's gas lamp, slightly unflattering at the angle her brother had nudged it. Either way, the mirror was a piece of glass. It had no commercial interest in her dissatisfaction.

A British fourteen-year-old in 2026 looks at herself in a mirror engineered by a multi-billion-pound algorithm whose entire business model depends on her dissatisfaction. The Instagram filter is not a piece of glass. It is an industry standing behind a piece of glass with a sales pitch.

The two mirrors are not the same. We are pretending they are.

[U — Urgency]

We are raising a generation in a hall of mirrors built by people they will never meet. The Mental Health Foundation's 2019 body-image report — its flagship piece of UK research on this issue — found thirty-four percent of British adults had felt anxious because of their body image, with the figure rising above half among teenagers. That research is now six years old; the technology has accelerated. The changing room before PE; the lunch queue; the bathroom photo retaken because the first one did not lie enough — each of these moments has industrialised since that survey was written. This is not vanity. This is a comparison engineered to fail.

[M1 — The platform]

Regulate the filter; not the child. Label the filtered photo the way we label the doctored one. Norway amended its Marketing Control Act in 2021 to require influencers and advertisers to disclose when an image of a body has been digitally edited; the legislation came into force in 2022, and the rest of Europe is still considering whether to follow. The British teenager scrolling tonight does not know which faces are altered and which are not — and the platforms that profit from her not knowing are not the bodies we should be asking to teach her. The platform must disclose. The child does not need to be re-educated; the image-makers do.

[M2 — The classroom]

Show the seam in the costume and the costume stops convincing. The Dove Self-Esteem Project, which delivers body-image curriculum content in over a hundred and fifty countries, has demonstrated that teaching teenagers how a filtered image is actually made — what is removed, what is added, what is smoothed, what is impossible — measurably reduces the comparison effect those images produce. A single PSHE lesson, taught with a phone and a screen and an editing demonstration, can take a year off the harm. The classroom can do what the platform will not.

[M3 — The voice]

Parents and teachers cannot out-shout the algorithm; they can teach the child to recognise it. Give her the spectacles, not just the mirror. PSHE Association guidance on media literacy and body image gives any tutor a starting script: how to ask, how to listen, how to name the trick the phone is playing. A fortnightly tutor-time conversation, repeated for a year, will not save the algorithm — but it will leave the teenager with a vocabulary the algorithm cannot take from her. The child who can name the lie is the child the lie cannot fully hold.

[V — Vision]

The mirror is honest again. The fourteen-year-old leaves the form room thinking about lunch, not her cheekbones. Childhood reclaims its own face.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say teenagers have always worried about looks — the filter is just the new mirror. There is a mirror, and there is a billion-pound industry standing behind that mirror with a sales pitch. The worry is not new. The seller is. To deny that distinction is to pretend the difference between a Victorian dressing-table glass and a global, profit-engineered comparison engine is a difference in degree, not in kind. It is not. The seller behind the mirror is the new fact.

[C — Conclusion]

Think about this last: the Victorian dressing-table mirror has been quietly replaced. The fourteen-year-old does not know it. The industry behind the new mirror does. We can either tell her — or we can leave her with the comparison and hope the next decade is kinder. The mirrors are not the same. Stop pretending they are. Give the mirror back to the child. The child is enough.

Alternative stems. The concrete-contrast hook fits all three — pick two unequal images and let them argue against each other.
