  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Less travels further."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (75–100 words · Think) — three options

  • Encouragement objection: "Recycling is still better than landfill; do not discourage it."
  • Consumer-economy objection: "Reducing consumption will collapse jobs in retail and manufacturing."
  • Class objection: "Telling families to consume less ignores the families that already cannot afford to consume more."
  • Refutation pivot: "Recycling is better than landfill — and reducing is better than recycling. The argument is not against the bin; it is against treating the bin as the end of the story when it is only the last page of a much longer one."

C — CONCLUSION (75–100 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the Tuesday-morning bin lorry; the proud kitchen.
  • New move: the crate is half-full; the kitchen has decided less; the lorry no longer carries an alibi.
  • Final sentence: "Stop polishing the bin. Reduce the bag."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (article for a national newspaper, ~720 words) — opens with a repetition / anaphora hook

[I — Introduction]

We have a recycling system that does not recycle. We have a sustainability story that does not sustain. We have a kitchen crate we believe is moral, a bin lorry we believe is the answer, an export ship to Malaysia we have agreed not to ask about, and a global figure — nine percent of all plastic ever produced — that the United Nations Environment Programme reported in 2023 and that we have agreed, collectively, to read past. We have built an economy that calls itself recycling and is, in fact, burying.

[U — Urgency]

We are confessing to bins that do not have the authority to forgive. WRAP's 2024 data show the United Kingdom places more than two million tonnes of plastic packaging on the market every year, and the UN Environment Programme reported in 2023 that globally only around nine percent of all plastic ever produced has actually been recycled. The IKEA shelf bought last year and binned this year; the H&M jumper worn twice; the takeaway box that arrives in five separate plastics — each of these flows through the confessional crate and out the other side, unforgiven. This is not sustainability. This is the kitchen's most expensive piece of theatre.

[M1 — Reduce]

We reduce, because reduction is the only verb in the climate vocabulary that actually works. WRAP's reduce-versus-recycle modelling has shown for over a decade that reduction outperforms recycling on every lifecycle measure that matters — carbon, water, landfill, downstream-export emissions. Recycling is an ambulance; reducing is the seatbelt. A single-purchase-per-household-item rule, applied for one year, would shift the country's plastic and textile arithmetic more than every Tuesday-morning kerbside collection combined. We reduce — because the first verb is the only one that counts.

[M2 — Repair]

We repair, because repair is the verb the high street used to know. Repair Cafés UK now host hundreds of free monthly events at which broken items are mended by volunteers. The European Union's Right to Repair Directive, agreed in 2024, requires manufacturers to offer repair services for ten years after a product is sold — a standard the United Kingdom has yet to adopt. Every kettle thrown away because the kettle was cheaper to replace than to mend is a small confession that we have lost the economy of repair. We repair — because the cobbler is the climate's friend.

[M3 — Refuse]

We refuse, because refusal is the moment of consumer ethics — and it does not happen at the bin. It happens at the till. Plastic Free July UK has tracked, through household participation data, that the simplest moral lever in the lifecycle is the moment of purchase, not the moment of disposal. Refuse the second plastic bag. Refuse the over-packaged delivery. Refuse the impulse buy that lasts a season and lands in landfill ten months later. We refuse — because the checkout is the polluter's audit.

[V — Vision]

The kitchen owns one bin. What is bought is repaired, repassed, and remade until it cannot be remade again. The high street has its cobbler. The Tuesday-morning lorry no longer carries an alibi. Less travels further.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say recycling is still better than landfill — and it is. Recycling is better than landfill; reducing is better than recycling. The argument is not against the bin. The argument is against treating the bin as the end of the story when it is only the last page of a much longer one. The kitchen that has only one bin is the kitchen that has done the chapter before the last.

[C — Conclusion]

We reduce. We repair. We refuse. We choose the verbs that actually move the lifecycle, not the ones that absolve the household. The bin lorry arrives on Tuesday. The crate is half-full. The country has decided less. The lorry no longer carries an alibi. We reduce — and the reduction is the country's longest-running, quietest, most ordinary climate policy. Stop polishing the bin. Reduce the bag.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter to a magazine on fast furniture as the next disaster) by anchoring M1 around furniture rather than packaging, and Stem 3 (speech to a community meeting telling adults to consume less) by re-tuning the I beat to a community-hall opening. The repetition hook ("We reduce / We repair / We refuse") carries cleanly across all three stems.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Confessional / alibi metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. No shows. No discourse markers.

Cross-question reuse table — memorise the recombinations

The ten plans above share underlying moves. The same metaphor frame, hook archetype, or refutation pivot reappears across multiple Qs. Memorise the recombinations and you can rebuild any plan from any other on the day.

Move Used in Reusable in
Casino / engineered-loss / dopamine-spend metaphor Q1 (Social media) Q6 (AI authenticity), Q10 (Consumer ethics)
Slow-flood / water-already-inside-the-room metaphor Q2 (Climate) Q3 (Animals), Q9 (Body image)
Victorian-museum / outlived-institution metaphor Q3 (Zoos) Q5 (Sport), Q10 (Consumer ethics)
Different-machine / different-clock framing Q4 (School hours) Q1 (Phones), Q9 (Body image)
Sandcastle-and-tide / overwhelmed-place metaphor Q7 (Tourism) Q2 (Climate), Q5 (Sport)
Funnelled-student / status-vs-purpose framing Q8 (Career) Q6 (AI in education), Q4 (Education)
Hall-of-mirrors / industrial-comparison framing Q9 (Body image) Q1 (Phones), Q10 (Consumer ethics)
Confessional-bin / alibi-as-clean-word framing Q10 (Consumer ethics) Q5 (Sport commercialisation), Q7 (Cruise tourism)
Counterintuitive opening fact Q1, Q5, Q6, Q9, Q10 Any
Sensory snapshot opening Q1, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q7, Q8, Q9, Q10 Any
Circular Conclusion echoing Introduction image All ten Mandatory shape across all 10
Triadic structure with short-sentence pivot All ten Mandatory shape across all 10

If exam time is tight, rehearse Q1, Q4, and Q6 first — the three with the highest 2026 probability score from the recurrence-cycle analysis. For broader coverage rehearse all ten. The shells are deliberately recombinant: on the day, the worst-case scenario is that AQA prints a stem you have not rehearsed; the best-case scenario is that the metaphor frame you rehearsed for Q3 fits the Q5 stem AQA actually printed. Both scenarios are designed for.

Creative stretches — one extreme example per Q

The ten skeletons above are the SAFE shape — IUMVCC, six beats, the locked structure that consistently scores AO5 7+. They will land your answer firmly in Grade 8.

To push into Grade 9 / 9, the examiner needs to see voice. Not just techniques — voice. The examples in The Complete Writer's Craft show the range: a conversational, self-interrupting essay that thinks aloud and is rigorously argued beneath the loose surface; a vivid, energetic description that builds one long controlled image; an argument made entirely through contrast. One Sophicly student once wrote a film review of The Hulk* in The Hulk's own voice — the inventive form was a perfect AO5 reward, because the form FIT the topic. None of these is a template to copy. They are proof that the opening can be anything, provided it earns its place.

The rule for breaking the rules. A creative stretch only works if the voice / form choice fits the topic. The Hulk voice can review a blockbuster; it cannot mourn a funeral or write to your headteacher. Always ask: does this voice ARGUE the topic, or does it just decorate it? If the voice argues, ship it. If it decorates, cut it. Never reach for a clever voice because it impressed you somewhere else — reach for the one this question needs.

Below, one stretch idea per Q. Use them as inspiration; do NOT mimic them verbatim.

Q1 — Social media: write as a smartphone keeping a diary

Stretch idea. Write the article from the point of view of a 14-year-old's smartphone, keeping a diary of its day. The phone records every notification it sent, every dopamine win it engineered, every conversation between the teenager and a parent it interrupted. By the closing entry, the phone is bored with its own victories.

Why it works. The form (article) is intact. The persona-shift (object monologue) does the argument the safe skeleton can only describe. The phone's quiet recording of harm is more damning than any statistic, because it comes from the device itself.

Risk to manage. Don't let the conceit collapse into novelty. The diary entries must still THINK — they must build an argument across the day, ending with the call to action implicit in the phone's own boredom.

Q2 — Climate: write as a 2050 weather forecaster

Stretch idea. Frame the speech as a 2050 BBC weather forecast. The forecaster reads out "Tuesday — forty-one degrees in Manchester, forty-three in London, breezy through Cornwall." The audience realises, slowly, that the forecast is a record from a different country. Bathos does the moral work: alongside the catastrophic numbers, the forecaster reminds viewers to "remember to peg the washing somewhere shady."

Why it works. Speech is the locked form. The forecast persona makes the abstract concrete — and the bathos (forty-three degrees + washing tips) does the moral work in a single line: the country has normalised the unsurvivable.

Risk to manage. The bathos must SERVE the argument, not undermine it. End the speech with the forecaster breaking character: "Or — we could choose a different forecast. We still have time to write the script of a different decade."

Q3 — Zoos: write as a parody Victorian tourist brochure

Stretch idea. Open the article as a verbatim 1898 brochure advertising London Zoo's new polar bear enclosure to the "discerning gentleman traveller". Three paragraphs of period-perfect Victorian advertising copy. Then, in paragraph four, the modern voice cuts in: "This is not a parody. This is the brochure they printed. We are still operating the institution it sold."

Why it works. AQA's 19th-century paired source archive is full of Victorian travel and natural-history writing — the pastiche is in conversation with the paper's own DNA. The shift from period voice to modern voice IS the argument: nothing has changed except the embarrassment.

Risk to manage. Period pastiche must sound authentic for two paragraphs minimum, otherwise the cut feels lazy. Read one Victorian zoo advert before the exam to land the register.

Q4 — School hours: write a letter from a 1860s factory owner

Stretch idea. Frame the letter to the Minister for Education as if dictated by a 1860s factory owner defending the 7:30am school bell as preparation for "the disciplines of industrial labour, sir, to which our boys and girls must be acclimatised from the earliest years." The student-modern voice cuts in at the close: "We received this letter in 1862. We are still acting on it in 2026."

Why it works. The pastiche makes the argument visible: the British school day is a Victorian artefact, and we keep operating it. The period voice does the polemic; the modern interruption lands the point.

Risk to manage. Period voice must be plausible Victorian (formal subordinate clauses, "Sir,", "I remain your humble servant"). One unintentionally modern phrase breaks the spell.

Q5 — Sport / commercialisation: write as a matchday ticket across the decades

Stretch idea. Personify a single seat — Row K, Seat 14 — and let it narrate the supporters who have sat in it across forty years. The 1985 occupant paid two pounds and queued in the rain; the 2025 occupant paid ninety and watched half the match through a phone screen, filming it for people who were not there. The seat notices what has changed about who can afford to sit in it.

Why it works. The object-narrator (apostrophe — a top-band AO5 technique) makes the abstraction "commercialisation" physical: the seat is the same; the price, the crowd and the meaning have changed around it. The argument arrives through what the seat has watched, not through assertion.

Risk to manage. Keep the seat's voice restrained — it observes, it does not lecture. The moment the seat starts giving you statistics in its own voice, the conceit breaks. Let the human detail (the rain, the phone screen, the empty corporate box) do the persuading.

Q6 — AI in education: write the first half AI-style, then break character

Stretch idea. Write the first three paragraphs in deliberate ChatGPT-bland — "In today's society, the rise of artificial intelligence has become an increasingly important topic. There are many things to consider when thinking about this issue, including the impact on students, teachers, and the wider community." Then, in paragraph four, the student voice arrives: "That paragraph took me four seconds. It took your concentration eight. We are now four paragraphs in and I have not made a single argument. This is what AI essays do."

Why it works. The meta move makes the argument by demonstrating it. AQA examiners read 200 of these answers; the one that opens by mocking the AI essay they were dreading is the one they remember.

Risk to manage. Only do this if you can sustain the bland AI voice for three paragraphs WITHOUT accidentally writing good prose. It must be flat, vague, full of "issues" and "impacts". Practice the bland.

Q7 — Tourism: write as the destination itself

Stretch idea. Personify Venice. Write the article as a letter FROM Venice TO the world. "I am Venice. I have been here for fifteen centuries. I am sinking, and I am also drowning in your queue. Please choose one."

The first-person voice of the city is sustained through every IUMVCC beat — Venice describes its own urgency, its own three-point methodology, its own counter-arguments to the cruise-ship industry's defence.

Why it works. Apostrophe (direct address from an absent / personified speaker) is a top-band AO5 technique. Personifying the place makes "overtourism" stop being an abstract noun and start being a body — and bodies persuade.

Risk to manage. Sustained personification is a marathon. The student voice cannot leak in. Stay in Venice's voice for the full 650 words, only breaking at the final call-to-action: "Travel less. Travel deeper. Pay Venice her rent."

Q8 — Career / vocation: write the speech as two job adverts read aloud

Stretch idea. Structure the opening as two job adverts read side by side. The first: "Wanted: someone to hold a ward together through a night shift. Pay: less, in real terms, than in 2010. Reward: a life that mattered." The second: "Wanted: someone to move money between accounts that already have too much of it. Pay: more than you can spend. Reward: to be decided." Then break out of the advert format into the IUMVCC argument.

Why it works. The paired-advert structure makes the contrast the whole country lives inside instantly visible — the same labour market, two incompatible value systems, read in the same breath. The form does the polemic before a single claim is made.

Risk to manage. The adverts must stay tight and parallel — same shape, opposite content. If the joke runs long it becomes a sketch, not an argument. Two short adverts, then out.

Q9 — Body image: write as the filter

Stretch idea. Persona-shift. Write the article from the point of view of the Instagram beauty filter, narrating its day shaping the faces of every fourteen-year-old who opens the app. The filter is enthusiastic, professional, slightly proud — until the closing paragraph, where it admits it has never met any of the faces it sells, and never will.

Why it works. Same logic as the Q1 smartphone-diary. The instrument of the harm narrates itself. The filter's cheerful professionalism is the indictment — examiner shock + AO5 inventive structure in one move.

Risk to manage. The filter's voice must not slide into the student's. The filter does not moralise — the filter SELLS. The argument lands by what the filter is too oblivious to see.

Q10 — Consumer ethics: write as the kitchen recycling crate

Stretch idea. First-person voice of the recycling crate on a Tuesday morning. The crate describes what it contains, where it is going (the lorry, the depot, the export ship to Malaysia, the open burn pit). The crate is matter-of-fact, slightly weary, knows the household believes the bin is a moral solution.

Why it works. Persona-shift to the object of the lie makes the household see itself from the outside. The bin's voice is dry, almost bored — which is the perfect tonal register for a system that absolves consumption while pretending to fix it.

Risk to manage. Do not let the crate become a Pixar character. Keep its voice flat, observational, undeceived. Closer to a coroner reading a cause-of-death than a children's-book narrator.

How to use these stretches in the exam

  • Do not attempt a stretch unless you have rehearsed it once. Cold improvisation in 45 minutes is risky.
  • The IUMVCC beats stay. Even the most inventive persona piece still has an introduction, an argument, a counter-argument, and a conclusion. The persona / voice is the surface; the structure underneath is unchanged.
  • One stretch only. Don't try to be the smartphone AND the filter AND the recycling crate. Pick one frame; sustain it.
  • If the topic doesn't fit, ship the safe skeleton. A bland Grade-8 answer beats a confused Grade-7 one every single time.

Final pre-submission checklist

Before you put the pen down, run this checklist in your head. Every item costs marks if it slips.

  • ✓ Six IUMVCC beats present and named in order.
  • ✓ Word count between 650 and 850. Under 650 = AO5 penalty; over 850 = control loses.
  • ✓ Hook from one of the eight archetypes — not a generic opening.
  • ✓ One extended metaphor anchored across at least three beats (I → U → Conclusion at minimum).
  • ✓ Triadic structure used with parallel grammar in U and Conclusion at minimum.
  • ✓ Conclusion echoes the Introduction image (circular structure rewards AO5).
  • ✓ One personal anecdote (in M only); no second anecdote.
  • ✓ Three rhetorical questions max, not more.
  • ✓ Direct address (you, we, us) anchored in I, V, Conclusion — not scattered through M.
  • ✗ No shows / showing / shown.
  • ✗ No abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to).
  • ✗ No discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly, Secondly, Finally).
  • ✗ No really, very, just, basically.
  • ✗ No Americanisms (move as noun, gotten, trash).
  • ✗ Fewer than ten to be verbs (is, are, was, were).
  • ✗ No more than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.

Tick every line. The exam is forty marks. The pen is the only thing between you and them.
